text: Here is the view of the right thoracic cavity.
We're going to perform minimally invasive turricoscopic esophagectomy.
So first we have to place our trocars.
This is the turricoscopic view of trocar placement.
For this surgery the patient is lying on the left side.
We see that the right lung is collapsed.
That is due to single lung ventilation being used during this procedure.
This is achieved with double lumen ventilation, which enables the collapse of one lung and ventilation of the other.
The surgeon begins the dissection with harmonic ACE device.
This is ultrasound device from the brand Eticon.
The right lower lobe is going to be mobilized.
As the dissection continues, we are approaching the hilus and we also see the pulsating heart.
The right lower lobe has to be mobilized in order to be able to perform a esophagectomy.
For better orientation, here we have a schematic picture of the important structures.
With the help of these landmarks and anatomical structures we now know where the esophagus is located.
The surgeon is now going to begin the dissection at the medial portion of the esophagus.
So as we see here the lung is being pulled cranially which is enabled by previous lung mobilization.
In this way we can get perfect exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon now begins the dissection medially from the esagus vein.
The dissection then continues cranially along the esagus vein.
We are now approaching the portion where the esagus vein is crossing over the esophagus.
Additional caution is necessary because this is the portion where the esagus vein is entering the superior vena cava and we do not want to cause any injuries to any of the structures.
The surgeon then continues the dissection of the medial portion of the esophagus and this is performed at the level of tracheal bifurcation.
It is also important to mention that all the lymph nodes that are laying in the surrounding tissue are going to be removed together with the specimen.
Harmonic device is really optimal instrument for this type of surgery since it doesn't only dissect the tissue but it also simultaneously provides hemostasis.
This way we are not experiencing any bleeding from the small vessels during the dissection.
We now see that the surgeon has moved to another portion of the surgery.
This is also our area of interest.
This is the area where the thoracic duct is located.
Thoracic duct lies pre-vertebrally and we have to make sure that we also take care of the thoracic duct in order to prevent formation of the chylotorax.
As you see here, the surgeon has switched up the instruments and is now dissecting the thoracic duct.
The surgeon now first has to circularly separate the thoracic duct from the surrounding tissue in order to be able to apply metal clips.
